stories.headline: Update: Cuba Condemns Trump's New Sanctions as 'Illegal,' Trump Threatens Military Presence
stories.summary:
Cuban Foreign Minister Bruno Rodriguez condemned a new wave of U.S. sanctions signed Friday by President Trump as "illegal and abusive," saying the measures violated the United Nations Charter and amounted to "collective punishment against the Cuban people." The executive order targets Cuban officials in the energy, defense, financial, and security sectors, as well as those alleged to have committed human rights abuses or corruption.

The announcement came as protesters marched outside the U.S. Embassy in Havana on International Workers Day, denouncing a U.S. blockade on oil to Cuba that has caused widespread blackouts and fuel shortages affecting hospitals, public transport, and schools. Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel, who had confirmed in March that Cuba was negotiating with Washington over bilateral relations, accused the U.S. of "intimidating and arrogant behaviour."

In a notably escalatory statement Friday evening in Florida, Trump told an audience that the U.S. would be "taking over" Cuba "almost immediately," suggesting an aircraft carrier could park offshore as a show of force. The remarks go beyond the previously covered executive order and represent a new public threat of potential military presence.

The U.S. and Cuba have maintained strained relations since Fidel Castro's 1959 revolution, with American trade and economic embargoes in place since 1960. Trump's latest moves come despite ongoing diplomatic back-channel contacts, raising questions about whether any negotiation track remains viable.

stories.headline: Nigeria Summons South African Envoy Over Attacks on Nigerian Nationals
stories.summary:
Nigeria has summoned South Africa's acting High Commissioner following a spate of attacks on foreign nationals in South Africa, the Nigerian foreign ministry announced. The formal meeting, scheduled for Monday, will address anti-immigrant marches and "documented instances of mistreatment of Nigerian citizens and attacks on their businesses."

At least two Nigerians and four Ethiopians have been killed in recent weeks, with anti-immigrant groups stopping people outside hospitals and schools to demand identity documents. During a march in Pretoria, foreign-owned businesses were urged to close out of fear of violence. South African President Cyril Ramaphosa has condemned the attacks but also cautioned foreigners to respect local laws, framing the issue as one of illegal immigration and alleged links to crime.

Nigeria warned that the incidents could affect existing bilateral relations between Africa's most populous nation and its most industrialized economy. Ghana summoned the South African envoy last month under similar circumstances after a video showed a Ghanaian man being challenged over his immigration papers.

South Africa is home to approximately 2.4 million documented migrants — less than 4% of the population — though the actual figure is believed to be higher. The tensions reflect long-running debates over immigration, unemployment, and regional solidarity across the African continent.

stories.headline: Massive Fire Destroys University of South Florida Marine Science Lab, Declared Total Loss
stories.summary:
A large fire broke out Saturday afternoon at the Marine Science Laboratory building on the University of South Florida's St. Petersburg campus, destroying the structure entirely. St. Petersburg Fire Chief Michael Lewis said more than 60 units and approximately 200 firefighters responded to the scene, and by approximately 9 p.m. declared the building a "total loss" with the entire roof burned off.

The building was evacuated safely and no injuries were reported. The cause of the fire remains under investigation. University alerts instructed students and staff to avoid the area as large plumes of gray smoke were visible rising from the campus.

The destruction of a dedicated marine science research facility represents a significant loss for the university's research programs, potentially affecting ongoing scientific work, equipment, data, and specimens housed in the lab. The full extent of research impact has not yet been assessed.

stories.headline: Democrats Mocked for Applauding King Charles After Participating in 'No Kings' Protests
stories.summary:
Republican lawmakers and commentators targeted Democratic politicians this week for enthusiastically welcoming Britain's King Charles III during his address to Congress, just weeks after many of the same Democrats had participated in or spoken at nationwide "No Kings" protests aimed at criticizing President Trump.

Rep. Mike Collins (R-Ga.) singled out Sen. Jon Ossoff (D-Ga.), who spoke at a "No Kings" rally in Savannah, for cheering Charles from the congressional floor. Rep. Greg Steube (R-Fla.) noted the absence of protest activity during the royal visit. Former New York City Mayor Rudolph Giuliani and actor Michael Rapaport, among others, circulated photos of Democrats including Rep. Ilhan Omar photographing the king with her phone.

Democrats and their allies have not broadly responded to the criticism, though the context differs: the "No Kings" protests were explicitly directed at what organizers described as executive overreach by President Trump, while the "No Kings Act" introduced by Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer targeted a Supreme Court ruling on presidential immunity. Supporters of the protests argue the slogan was always directed at Trump's conduct rather than monarchies generally.

King Charles completed a state visit to the United States that included a White House dinner with President Trump and the address to a joint session of Congress, events seen as an effort to reinforce the U.S.-U.K. special relationship.

stories.headline: Update: Murder Charge Filed in Death of 5-Year-Old Australian Girl; Riots Follow Arrest
stories.summary:
Jefferson Lewis, 47, has been charged with murder and two counts of sexual assault following the death of a five-year-old Aboriginal girl identified by her family as Kumanjayi Little Baby. Her body was discovered Thursday after a major multi-day police search in Alice Springs, Northern Territory. Northern Territory Police Commissioner Martin Dole announced the charges Sunday morning and urged the community to respect the judicial process. Lewis is scheduled to appear in a Darwin court Tuesday.

The arrest triggered violent riots outside the Alice Springs hospital where Lewis was being treated for injuries he sustained before being taken into custody. Crowds threw projectiles at police, set at least one police vehicle ablaze, and looted a nearby petrol station and supermarket. Police responded with tear gas and subsequently transferred Lewis roughly 1,500 kilometers north to Darwin for his own safety. Five people have been arrested in connection with the riots, and property damage is estimated at more than A$180,000.

The girl's grandfather, senior Warlpiri elder Robin Granites, called for calm, saying the community's anger was understandable but that 'this is not our way.' Commissioner Dole described the riot behavior as 'disgusting' and 'abhorrent,' drawing a sharp distinction between genuine grief and criminal conduct. The case has drawn national attention in Australia to both child safety in remote Aboriginal communities and the outbreak of civil unrest that followed.

stories.headline: Austria Arrests Suspect in Rat Poison Baby Food Tampering Case
stories.summary:
Austrian police have detained a 39-year-old suspect in connection with the discovery of rat poison in HiPP baby food jars sold at SPAR supermarkets in eastern Austria. The Burgenland State Criminal Police Office launched the investigation after a tampered jar was reported by a customer in the city of Eisenstadt on April 18. Five tampered jars were ultimately seized before any were consumed; none of the targeted products were eaten and no injuries have been reported.

The case spans three countries: HiPP, a German-based organic baby food company, recalled all of its baby food jars from SPAR-affiliated stores in Austria as a precaution, and retailers in Slovakia and the Czech Republic also pulled the brand's jars from shelves. The affected product was a 6.7-ounce jar of carrot-and-potato food formulated for infants as young as five months. HiPP stated the jars left its facility in 'perfect condition' and said it had been a 'victim of extortion,' noting that an unidentified blackmailer had sent a message to a shared mailbox.

The Burgenland public prosecutor's office has opened an investigation into 'intentional endangerment of the public.' An expert toxicological report on the poison's potency is still pending. HiPP said it was 'greatly relieved' by the arrest and pledged further updates as verified details emerge.

stories.headline: Update: Prosecutors Release Video of Suspect Storming White House Correspondents' Dinner
stories.summary:
Federal prosecutors released video footage Thursday showing the moment Cole Tomas Allen, a California man, ran through a security magnetometer armed with a long gun and knives at the White House Correspondents' Association dinner on April 25. U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro posted the video on social media amid ongoing questions about whether a Secret Service officer struck in the melee was hit by Allen's weapon or by friendly fire. Pirro stated there is no evidence the officer was hit by a colleague's bullet.

The footage appears to show Allen pointing his weapon at a Secret Service agent, who then fired back five times. Allen was injured during the incident but was not shot. He appeared briefly in federal court Thursday, agreed to remain jailed pending trial, and did not enter a plea. The attack disrupted one of the most high-profile annual gatherings in Washington, which was attended by journalists, administration officials, and others.

Secret Service Director Sean Curran defended the agency's security planning for the event and said he would not change the protocols. The release of the video marks a significant new development in the case, providing the first visual account of the attack and partially addressing the disputed question of how the officer was wounded. The suspect faces federal charges related to the alleged assassination attempt on President Trump.

stories.headline: Medicare Portal Inadvertently Published Social Security Numbers of Health Providers
stories.summary:
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services unintentionally published Social Security numbers for at least 100 health providers on its publicly accessible National Provider Directory, a database used by seniors to locate healthcare professionals. The agency took down the portal after The Washington Post alerted CMS officials to the exposure. The scope of how long the data was publicly visible or whether it was accessed by unauthorized parties has not been disclosed.

The incident raises fresh concerns about data security practices within federal health agencies at a time when the Department of Government Efficiency has been conducting sweeping reviews of federal IT systems. Privacy advocates and healthcare industry groups have previously warned that government provider databases contain sensitive personal information that, if exposed, could facilitate identity theft or fraud.

CMS has not publicly detailed how the Social Security data came to be included in the directory or what remediation steps are being taken beyond removing the page. The episode adds to a pattern of government data exposure incidents that have drawn scrutiny from both parties in Congress.
